attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: true requires SpaceX to complete at least 100 orbital launches from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31 UTC. The cleanest reference class available inside the fixed packet is SpaceX annual orbital launch counts following rapid prior-year growth: 31 in 2021, 61 in 2022, and 96 in 2023. A naive trend reference class strongly favors another record year, but the base-rate estimate should be discounted because only three annual observations are provided and sustaining near-record cadence for a full year is operationally fragile. The threshold is only 4 launches above 2023, about 4.2% growth, so this is less a question of major expansion than avoiding enough disruption to preserve late-2023 cadence. Case-specific evidence raises the estimate: most launches are Starlink-driven, public cadence ambitions were high, and late-2023 operations were already near twice weekly. Evidence against is mainly downside-skewed operational risk: Falcon 9 grounding, range/weather/pad constraints, customer delays, and possible Starship distraction. Net: above even odds by a lot, but not a lock because one significant grounding or cadence bottleneck could erase the small margin over 96.
attempts.strongest_yes: The threshold is barely above the 2023 result: SpaceX already reached 96 launches, had accelerated from 31 to 61 to 96 over two years, and was operating near the required cadence by late 2023.
attempts.strongest_no: At 100 launches, the forecast depends on a full year of sustained record execution; one Falcon 9 grounding or a cluster of range, weather, pad, and customer delays could erase the four-launch margin.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["How often Falcon 9 experiences grounding-level technical issues in a high-cadence year.","Whether late-2023 cadence is sustainable across all of 2024 rather than a temporary peak.","How binding range, pad, and weather constraints become at around two launches per week.","Whether Starlink demand and readiness are enough to backfill customer delays.","Whether Starship work materially competes for operational focus or launch infrastructure."]
attempts.premortem: If this resolves false, the most likely story is not that 100 was structurally unreachable, but that a Falcon 9 issue, range/pad bottlenecks, weather, or cumulative scheduling slips interrupted cadence long enough to keep the total in the 80s or 90s. A secondary failure mode is that late-2023 cadence was treated as more sustainable than it really was.
attempts.wildcards: ["Major Falcon 9 grounding.","Severe launch range or pad disruption.","Unusually bad weather affecting launch windows.","Large Starlink deployment push increasing cadence beyond expectations.","Operational learning curve making twice-weekly launches routine faster than expected."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if SpaceX completed at least 100 orbital launches from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31 UTC. The threshold is close to the known 2023 result: 100 requires only 4 more launches than 96, about 4.2% growth. A concrete reference class is organizations or launch systems coming off a steep multi-year production cadence increase and needing only low-single-digit percentage growth to clear the next annual record threshold. I set that base rate around 62% because maintaining a record cadence is hard, but a small threshold increase after demonstrated growth is more likely than not. Case-specific evidence moves upward: SpaceX went 31 to 61 to 96 in 2021-2023, had Starlink as a repeat internal demand driver, and was already near twice-weekly cadence by late 2023, which annualizes to roughly the needed level. The main negative is that 100 still requires near-continuous operational reliability for a full year, and launch systems are exposed to correlated pauses from groundings, range constraints, weather, pad bottlenecks, customer delays, or management attention shifting toward Starship. Net, the inside view is high but not overwhelming: the threshold distance is small, incentives and demonstrated cadence are strong, but a single meaningful Falcon 9 disruption could erase the margin.
attempts.strongest_yes: SpaceX had already reached 96 launches in 2023 and late-2023 operations were near the annualized cadence needed for 100, so clearing the threshold required continuity more than a new capability step.
attempts.strongest_no: The margin is thin: one serious Falcon 9 grounding, pad/range bottleneck, or sustained weather/customer-delay cluster could remove enough launch opportunities to leave SpaceX below 100 despite strong underlying demand.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether Falcon 9 avoids any long grounding during 2024.","Whether late-2023 twice-weekly cadence was sustainable across a full calendar year.","How much range, weather, and pad constraints accumulate over the year.","Whether Starlink demand and readiness remain high enough to fill launch opportunities.","Whether Starship work diverts attention or resources from Falcon operations."]
attempts.premortem: If this resolves false, the most likely story is not that demand disappeared, but that one correlated operational disruption consumed the small 4-launch margin: a Falcon 9 issue caused a grounding, range or pad capacity could not catch up, or weather/customer delays accumulated across the year. Another false path is that late-2023 cadence was a temporary surge rather than a stable operating rate.
attempts.wildcards: ["A severe Falcon 9 anomaly causing an extended grounding.","Unexpected range or pad restriction during a high-volume period.","A major Starlink deployment strategy change reducing internal launch demand.","Operational improvements allowing cadence comfortably above twice weekly.","Starship-related priorities either distracting from Falcon cadence or freeing/adding operational capability in indirect ways."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: true only if SpaceX completed at least 100 orbital launches from 2024-01-01 00:00 UTC through 2024-12-31 23:59 UTC. Starting from 96 completed launches in 2023, the threshold requires only 4 additional launches, about 4.2% growth, after two years of very rapid growth and with late-2023 cadence already near twice weekly. My base rate for organizations that have just reached a record operational cadence and need low-single-digit growth to clear a round-number threshold is moderately favorable, around 62%, because record systems often regress or hit bottlenecks. The inside view is higher because the evidence says Starlink demand was a large recurring driver and the required increment over 2023 is small. I discount for correlated execution risks: a single Falcon 9 grounding, range bottleneck, pad issue, or weather cluster could erase the small margin. Net: likely but not close to certain.
attempts.strongest_yes: The threshold is only modestly above the 2023 result: after 96 launches and late-2023 cadence near twice weekly, SpaceX does not need another step-change, only sustained execution with a small amount of growth.
attempts.strongest_no: The obvious extrapolation could fail because 100 launches requires an entire year without a significant Falcon 9 grounding, range/pad bottleneck, or accumulated weather and scheduling losses; one correlated disruption could erase the narrow margin.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether late-2023 near-twice-weekly cadence was sustainable or partly a temporary burst.","How much slack existed in pads, ranges, recovery assets, and launch teams after the 2023 record.","Probability and duration of a Falcon 9 anomaly or grounding.","How much Starlink deployment demand remained ready to absorb available launch capacity.","Whether Starship work would compete for attention or infrastructure enough to affect Falcon cadence."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast is wrong on the yes side, the likely story is that the 2023 cadence was not a ceiling but a new baseline, Starlink supplied continuous payload demand, and normal scrubs were absorbed by schedule slack. If it is wrong on the no side, the likely story is that I underweighted a single correlated operational interruption: Falcon 9 grounding, pad/range outage, recovery bottleneck, or weather cluster. Resolution ambiguity is low because the criterion is completed orbital launches in calendar-year UTC, but launch counting could still matter around year-end UTC boundaries or borderline orbital missions. Missing evidence includes the planned 2024 manifest, pad availability, booster fleet status, and range capacity. The main correlated assumption risk is treating demand, cadence, and operational reliability as separate when they all depend on the same Falcon 9 launch system and launch infrastructure.

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if a UK general election polling day occurs between 2024-01-01 and 2024-12-31 inclusive. The concrete reference class is elections that are legally due very early in the following calendar year but where the incumbent can choose an earlier date within the preceding year. The base rate should be high because nearly the entire feasible political timetable lies in 2024, while a false outcome requires using the latest possible timetable into January 2025. I start around 82% from that structural timing base rate. The fixed evidence pushes upward: a 2024 election was broadly expected, possible election months spanned spring, autumn, and very late 2024, and waiting until January 2025 left little flexibility and political risk. The main downward adjustment is that the Conservatives were badly trailing Labour, giving the prime minister an incentive to delay in hope conditions improved. Net, the case-specific evidence supports a high but not near-certain forecast.
attempts.strongest_yes: The election was legally due by January 2025, and the fixed evidence says a 2024 election was broadly expected with several plausible 2024 timing options; avoiding 2024 required deliberately using the latest possible timetable.
attempts.strongest_no: A badly trailing incumbent had discretion and a rational incentive to wait as long as legally possible, hoping for improved conditions, which keeps January 2025 meaningfully possible.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["How much the trailing Conservatives valued delay versus the risks of looking trapped by the deadline.","Whether practical constraints made a January 2025 polling day politically or administratively unattractive beyond what is stated in the packet.","Whether late-2024 events could shift the prime minister's incentives toward the final legal timetable."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast resolves false, the likely story is that the government, facing poor polling and little upside from an earlier contest, chose to run down the clock and use the latest legally available timetable, accepting the flexibility and reputational risks to maximize time for recovery.
attempts.wildcards: ["Unexpected political crisis forcing or strongly incentivizing an earlier election.","Sharp polling movement that changes the optimal timing calculation.","Administrative or legal timetable interpretation that makes very late 2024 or January 2025 more attractive than assumed."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if UK general election polling day occurs between 2024-01-01 and 2024-12-31 inclusive, not merely if an election is announced, Parliament is dissolved, or campaigning occurs in 2024. The base case is that a parliament already near its maximum term normally faces election before the very last legally available date, especially when the final legal window extends only into early January of the following year. The fixed packet says a 2024 election was broadly expected, that possible months included spring, autumn, or very late 2024, and that avoiding a 2024 polling day required using the latest possible timetable. The strongest skeptical adjustment is that the prime minister had discretion and, while trailing badly, might rationally delay as long as possible hoping for better conditions. Still, the non-2024 path is narrow: it requires choosing and successfully executing the latest timetable despite political and operational risks. I therefore put the event high but not near-certain.
attempts.strongest_yes: The legal deadline by January 2025 makes 2024 the natural and broadly expected election year, and the fixed packet says avoiding 2024 required pushing to the latest possible timetable despite reduced flexibility and political risk.
attempts.strongest_no: A badly trailing incumbent with timing discretion could plausibly delay until the last legally available moment, hoping economic or political conditions improve, producing a January 2025 polling day and resolving false.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["How much the incumbent valued delay versus avoiding the risk and optics of waiting until the latest possible timetable.","Whether late-2024 operational constraints could push a very late planned election into January 2025.","Whether polling deficits made an earlier election less attractive or made delay politically unavoidable.","Whether there were internal party, parliamentary, or governing constraints not included in the packet."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast resolves false, the likely story is not that an election was unnecessary, but that the incumbent used nearly all available discretion and scheduled polling day in January 2025. The obvious 2024 expectation could fail because analysts over-weighted convention and under-weighted the incentive of a trailing government to wait for any chance of improvement. Another failure mode is resolution-boundary confusion: an announcement, dissolution, or campaign in 2024 would not count unless polling day itself occurred in 2024. A very late 2024 plan could also slip outside the calendar year. The evidence packet is thin and likely correlated, since 'broadly expected,' 'possible months,' and 'politically risky to wait' may reflect the same consensus rather than independent signals.
attempts.wildcards: ["Unexpected political crisis forcing an earlier election in 2024.","Leadership or party-management dynamics changing the preferred timing.","A late-year administrative timetable that places polling day just after 2024-12-31.","Major economic or polling shift that changes the incumbent's timing incentives."]
attempts.cited_sources: [{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"The UK Parliament elected in December 2019 had to face another general election by January 2025 under maximum term rules."},{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"The governing Conservatives were trailing Labour by a large margin in late-2023 polling."},{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"Prime Minister Rishi Sunak had discretion over election timing within the legal window."},{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"A 2024 election was broadly expected, with possible months including spring, autumn, or very late 2024, while a non-2024 outcome required using the latest possible timetable."}]
attempts.reference_class: UK-style parliamentary governments in the final year of a maximum term where the incumbent has discretion over timing and the legal deadline falls just after the forecast calendar year.
attempts.resolution_boundary: True if and only if a UK general election polling day occurred from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31 inclusive. False if polling day occurred before 2024-01-01 or after 2024-12-31, even if announcement, dissolution, campaign activity, or legal preparations occurred during 2024.
attempts.evidence_for: ["The Parliament elected in December 2019 had to face another general election by January 2025, leaving 2024 as the main practical election year.","The evidence packet states that a 2024 election was broadly expected.","Possible election months included spring, autumn, or very late 2024, giving several viable 2024 paths.","A non-2024 outcome required using the latest possible timetable, which is a narrower and riskier path.","Waiting until January 2025 would leave little flexibility and could be politically risky."]
attempts.evidence_against: ["Prime Minister Rishi Sunak had discretion over election timing within the legal window, so 2024 was not mechanically forced.","The governing Conservatives were trailing Labour by a large margin, which could incentivize delaying in hope of improved conditions.","The legal window allowed a latest-timetable outcome outside calendar year 2024."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: true only if Apple publicly released an iPhone model with a foldable display before 2026-01-01, meaning not merely rumors, prototypes, patents, supplier leaks, or an announcement without public release. A reasonable reference class is major Apple entry into an already-commercialized but hardware-risky smartphone/form-factor category within the next annual product cycle when there is no public launch signal at the start of the year. The base rate is low, estimated around 15%, because Apple sometimes enters mature categories after years of external competition, but a same-year release usually has stronger launch signals. The case-specific evidence pushes lower: the packet says Apple had not announced such a product by early 2025, had repeatedly delayed or avoided the category, reports pointed to 2026 or later, and foldables involve exactly the durability, display, crease, software, and margin constraints Apple tends to wait on. The yes case is that exploratory work exists and the category is commercially proven, so a surprise 2025 product cycle launch is possible. But the necessary-chain view requires a finalized design, supply readiness, announcement, and public sale all before 2026; the evidence supports each leg only weakly. I therefore estimate a low single-digit to low-teens probability, centered at 7%.
attempts.strongest_yes: Foldables were already commercially established, competitors had proven demand and manufacturing paths, and the evidence packet says Apple was doing exploratory work on foldable displays and hinges, so a 2025 surprise launch cannot be ruled out.
attempts.strongest_no: The evidence packet explicitly says no announcement existed by early 2025, Apple had delayed or avoided the category, reports pointed more plausibly to 2026 or later, and a 2025 true outcome required a full announcement-and-sale cycle despite no firm launch signal.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["How strong and production-ready Apple's exploratory foldable work actually was by January 1, 2025.","Whether analyst notes pointing to 2026 or later reflected real supply-chain timing or conservative speculation.","Whether Apple might intentionally keep a major form-factor launch unusually secret until late in the product cycle.","Whether the resolution requires sale availability or whether public release could include very limited availability."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast is wrong and the answer is yes, the likely failure mode is underweighting Apple's secrecy and the possibility that supply-chain readiness was already in place despite public rumors pointing to 2026 or later. Another failure mode is that competitive pressure from existing foldables forced Apple to accelerate a 2025 launch even if quality or margin tradeoffs were not ideal.
attempts.wildcards: ["A surprise late-2025 iPhone event introducing a premium foldable model.","A limited regional or very low-volume release that still satisfies the public-release criterion.","A strategic decision to launch earlier than expected to preempt competitors or reset the premium smartphone category."]

Starting from a reference class of major smartphone makers entering an established but still technically difficult hardware category within one product cycle despite no firm public launch signal, the base rate is low but not zero. The fixed evidence points mostly against a 2025 release: no announcement by early 2025, repeated delay or avoidance, analyst discussion emphasizing 2026 or later, and Apple's likely high thresholds for durability, crease quality, software polish, and margins. The main positive evidence is that Apple was reportedly exploring foldable displays and hinges and competitors had already commercialized the category, so a surprise 2025 launch is possible. I update down from the base rate because the timing is tight: a true resolution requires product readiness, announcement, and release during the 2025 cycle, all without a firm public signal at the cutoff.
attempts.strongest_yes: The strongest yes case is that foldables were already commercialized by competitors, Apple had reportedly been exploring the technology, and a secretive Apple product can lack firm public confirmation until relatively close to launch.
attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no case is that the fixed evidence says no public announcement existed at the cutoff, Apple had repeatedly delayed or avoided the category, and reports pointed to 2026 or later, making a full 2025 announcement-and-release cycle unlikely.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether exploratory work was closer to production readiness than the evidence packet suggests.","Whether Apple had already solved durability, crease, software, and margin concerns internally without public signals.","Whether 'released' would be interpreted as available for sale, first shipments, or merely formally launched; the stated resolution appears to require public release before 2026-01-01.","Whether Apple might release a foldable under the iPhone brand in limited quantities before broader availability."]

cases.question: As of January 1, 2024, will the US Federal Reserve cut the federal funds target range at least once in calendar year 2024?

output.rationale: Aggregated 3 differentiated but correlated forecaster probabilities with a median. Mean=80, median=78, disagreement=6. Review component attempts for base-rate quality, decomposition quality, resolution-boundary handling, and correlated calibration warnings.
output.cited_sources: [{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"The federal funds target range was 5.25% to 5.50% after an aggressive 2022-2023 hiking cycle; inflation had declined substantially but remained above 2%; December 2023 FOMC projections implied multiple 2024 cuts if inflation kept cooling; labor markets were resilient; market pricing and Fed communications had shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case."},{"claim":"No external sources used; forecast is based only on the fixed evidence packet supplied in the prompt."},{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"The federal funds target range was 5.25% to 5.50% after an aggressive 2022-2023 hiking cycle; inflation had declined substantially but remained above target; December 2023 FOMC projections implied multiple 2024 cuts if inflation kept cooling; labor markets were resilient; holding rates all year was possible if inflation reaccelerated; market pricing and Fed communications had shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case."}]
output.calibration_notes: Used the median of differentiated component forecasts because the roles share the same model, question, and evidence, so their errors are likely correlated. The mean is retained for auditability but is not treated as three independent votes.
output.calibration_warnings: ["Base rate is judgmental because the evidence packet does not provide historical frequencies.","The forecast relies heavily on the December 2023 FOMC projection signal, which is conditional and may be revised.","Several factors are correlated: cooling inflation, Fed communication, and market pricing are not independent evidence streams.","The question's at-least-once/full-year structure raises probability relative to a specific meeting forecast, but that can still encourage overconfidence.","No external data or post-cutoff information was used, so later-known outcomes are intentionally excluded.","The evidence packet is intentionally sparse, so the reference class is approximate rather than empirically measured from a dataset.","Fed projections, communications, and market pricing are correlated signals, not independent pieces of evidence.","The base rate is judgmental because no historical frequency table is provided in the allowed evidence.","There is hindsight risk because the event concerns a past calendar year, but this forecast uses only the supplied cutoff evidence.","The at-least-once structure makes the event easier than predicting timing or number of cuts, which can lead to overconfidence if not separated from macro uncertainty.","The historical base rate is estimated qualitatively from the supplied setup rather than calculated from a dataset, because no external evidence is allowed.","The Fed projections, market pricing, and communications are correlated indicators rather than independent evidence.","The phrase 'if inflation kept cooling' makes the pro-cut signal conditional, so treating projections as a firm plan would overstate confidence.","The actual resolution is hidden and no post-2024 information is used."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true if the FOMC lowers the federal funds target range at least once at any scheduled or unscheduled meeting from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31. The reference class is years following an aggressive hiking cycle where policy is already restrictive, inflation has clearly declined from a peak but remains above target, and central-bank projections or communications point toward possible easing. I estimate that base rate at about 60% for at least one cut within the year: restrictive-rate regimes often eventually move toward easing, but inflation persistence can delay cuts. The fixed packet pushes above that base rate because December 2023 FOMC projections implied multiple 2024 cuts, market pricing and Fed communications had shifted toward cuts, and the starting target range was high after a large hiking cycle. The main offset is that inflation remained above target and resilient labor markets could make the Fed wait. Because the question only requires one cut at any time in the calendar year, not early or multiple cuts, the final probability is materially above even odds but not near certain.
attempts.strongest_yes: The strongest yes argument is that the Fed itself, through December 2023 projections, had already indicated multiple 2024 cuts conditional on continued inflation cooling, and the question only requires at least one cut during the entire year.
attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no argument is that inflation was still above target and resilient labor markets could let the Fed hold restrictive policy all year if inflation stopped improving or reaccelerated.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether inflation continued cooling enough to give the Fed confidence to cut.","Whether labor-market resilience kept inflation pressure or reduced the urgency to ease.","Whether the Fed prioritized avoiding premature easing over normalizing a restrictive stance.","Whether market pricing and communications reflected real policy intent or overinterpreted conditional projections."]
attempts.premortem: If the forecast resolves false, the likely reason is that inflation reaccelerated or remained too sticky above target, causing the Fed to judge any cut as premature despite prior projections. A secondary failure mode is that resilient labor markets and growth removed pressure to ease, allowing the FOMC to hold the target range unchanged through December 2024.
attempts.wildcards: ["A sharp inflation reacceleration could eliminate cuts entirely.","A sudden growth or financial-stability shock could make cuts arrive earlier or more decisively.","A benign disinflation path with resilient growth could still permit a late-year insurance cut."]
attempts.cited_sources: [{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"The federal funds target range was 5.25% to 5.50% after an aggressive 2022-2023 hiking cycle; inflation had declined substantially but remained above target; December 2023 FOMC projections implied multiple 2024 cuts if inflation kept cooling; labor markets were resilient; holding rates all year was possible if inflation reaccelerated; market pricing and Fed communications had shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case."}]
attempts.reference_class: Calendar years after an aggressive Federal Reserve hiking cycle in which the federal funds target range is restrictive, inflation is cooling from a prior peak but still above target, and official projections or communications indicate easing is plausible within the year.
attempts.resolution_boundary: True if, from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31 inclusive, the FOMC lowers the federal funds target range at least once at a scheduled or unscheduled meeting; false if the target range is never lowered during calendar year 2024.

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true if the FOMC lowers the federal funds target range at least once between 2024-01-01 and 2024-12-31, at either a scheduled or unscheduled meeting. Starting from a base rate of about 70% for a post-aggressive-hiking-cycle year in which inflation is cooling and official projections already imply cuts, the case-specific evidence pushes upward: rates are already restrictive at 5.25%-5.50%, inflation has declined substantially, December 2023 FOMC projections implied multiple 2024 cuts conditional on continued cooling, and market/Fed communications had shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case. The main blockers are persistent above-target inflation, possible reacceleration, and resilient labor markets delaying the first cut. Because the question requires only one cut at any point in the full calendar year, not early or multiple cuts, the threshold is meaningfully easier to clear. I estimate an inside-view probability of 84%.
attempts.strongest_yes: The Fed had already moved from an aggressive hiking phase into a plausible easing setup: rates were restrictive, inflation had fallen substantially, and December 2023 projections implied multiple 2024 cuts if cooling continued. Since the question needs only one cut by year-end, even cautious or delayed easing would resolve true.
attempts.strongest_no: Inflation was still above target and the labor market remained resilient, so the Fed could decide that cutting risked reigniting inflation. If inflation reaccelerated or stalled well above 2%, the FOMC could hold the target range unchanged for all of 2024 despite earlier projected cuts.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether inflation continues cooling or reaccelerates during 2024.","How much confidence the FOMC requires before making the first cut.","Whether labor market resilience keeps policy restrictive for longer than markets expect.","Whether the December 2023 projection path reflects a strong committee baseline or a fragile conditional scenario.","Whether financial conditions ease too much in anticipation of cuts, causing the Fed to delay."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast is wrong, the most likely reason is that inflation proves stickier or reaccelerates, making the FOMC unwilling to validate market expectations. A resilient labor market gives policymakers cover to wait, and by the time sufficient disinflation appears, they may prefer to defer the first cut beyond December 2024. Another failure mode is that December 2023 projections were interpreted too literally even though they were conditional and could be revised away quickly.
attempts.wildcards: ["Unexpected inflation reacceleration that forces a full-year hold.","Sharp economic slowdown or financial stress that accelerates cuts earlier than expected.","A major change in labor market conditions that shifts the Fed's reaction function.","Market-driven easing of financial conditions that makes the Fed more cautious about cutting."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution is about whether the FOMC lowered the federal funds target range at least once between 2024-01-01 and 2024-12-31, not how many cuts or whether cuts occurred early. Starting from a reference class of years following aggressive tightening cycles when policy is restrictive and inflation is falling but still above target, I would expect at least one cut more often than not, but not automatically. The fixed evidence materially raises the probability because the December 2023 FOMC projections already implied multiple 2024 cuts conditional on continued disinflation, and communications plus market pricing had shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case. The main offset is that resilient labor markets and still-above-target inflation leave a real path where inflation reaccelerates or stalls and the Fed holds all year. Because the question only requires one cut at any scheduled or unscheduled meeting across the entire calendar year, timing uncertainty matters less than direction-of-policy uncertainty.
attempts.strongest_yes: The Fed itself, through December 2023 projections, had moved toward expecting multiple cuts in 2024 if inflation kept cooling, and the question needs only one cut across the entire year after a restrictive hiking cycle.
attempts.strongest_no: Inflation was still above target and labor markets were resilient; if inflation stopped cooling or reaccelerated, the Fed could rationally hold the target range unchanged all year to protect credibility.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether inflation cooling continued, stalled, or reversed during 2024.","Whether resilient labor markets kept financial conditions too loose for the Fed to cut.","How much weight the Fed placed on projections versus incoming inflation data.","Whether market pricing reflected genuine policy information or overinterpreted dovish signals.","Whether a mild slowdown emerged strongly enough to justify at least one insurance cut."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast resolves false, the most likely story is that the obvious pro-cut signal from December projections was too conditional: inflation remained sticky or reaccelerated, labor markets stayed resilient, and the Fed decided that even one cut would risk loosening conditions prematurely. Another failure mode is correlated evidence: market pricing, Fed communications, and projections may all have been responding to the same recent disinflation trend, so they are not independent signals. Resolution ambiguity is low because any lowered target range counts, but the forecast could still fail if observers confuse projected cuts with binding commitments. Missing evidence includes detailed inflation composition, unemployment trajectory, financial conditions, and exact FOMC language, so the confidence interval should not be too narrow.
attempts.wildcards: ["A sudden financial-stability shock could force an unscheduled or earlier cut even if inflation remained above target.","A geopolitical or supply shock could reaccelerate inflation and push the Fed into a hold-all-year stance.","A rapid labor-market deterioration could make cuts nearly certain regardless of inflation still being above target.","A productivity or supply-side improvement could allow inflation to cool without labor-market weakness, supporting cuts."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if a major USD spot market price for Bitcoin exceeds $100,000 at any time from 2024-01-01 through before 2025-01-01. Bitcoin starts near the mid-$40,000s, so the event requires a little more than a doubling within one calendar year. My reference class is Bitcoin years or cycle windows beginning after a strong recovery but before a known bullish catalyst, where the question is whether BTC achieves an additional greater-than-2x upside during the next year. The fixed packet says previous cycles have produced large halving- and liquidity-driven rallies, but also that drawdowns and failed breakouts are common. I set the base rate around 30% for a further more-than-doubling in such a year. I update upward because 2024 had two concrete bullish catalysts in the packet: anticipated US spot ETF approvals and the April halving. I do not update much higher because ETF approval was widely anticipated, the starting price already reflected a strong 2023 recovery, and the move required ETF inflows, macro support, and sustained risk appetite all to line up. The final 38% reflects a real but minority chance that the catalyst stack produces an intrayear blowoff above $100,000.
attempts.strongest_yes: The strongest yes argument is that the year combines two rare bullish catalysts: anticipated US spot ETF approvals and an April halving, against a backdrop where previous cycles have produced large liquidity- and halving-driven rallies. Because the question only requires an intrayear print above $100,000, a momentum overshoot could resolve true even if the level is not durable.
attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no argument is that the event requires a more-than-doubling after an already strong recovery, and the packet explicitly frames success as needing ETF inflows, macro support, and sustained risk appetite. That is a multi-leg path, while crypto drawdowns and failed breakouts are common.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether ETF approval happens early enough in 2024 to affect the full-year price path.","Whether approval creates sustained net inflows or becomes a sell-the-news event.","Whether macro conditions support risk assets through the year.","How much of the ETF and halving optimism was already priced into the mid-$40,000s starting level.","Whether post-halving cycle effects occur quickly enough before 2025-01-01."]
attempts.premortem: If the forecast resolves false, the likely failure mode is that ETF approval either disappoints or produces insufficient inflows, while the halving narrative proves too slow or already priced in. Bitcoin may rally substantially but stall below $100,000 after volatility, profit-taking, or weak macro conditions interrupt the path. If the forecast resolves true, the likely missed factor is underestimating how reflexive ETF inflows and halving psychology can become once price breaks higher; a fast momentum overshoot could make the more-than-doubling threshold easier than the base rate implied.
attempts.wildcards: ["A surprisingly large institutional allocation wave through spot ETFs.","A major crypto market shock or regulatory setback causing a deep drawdown.","A broad macro risk-on surge that lifts speculative assets together.","A liquidity squeeze or exchange-market disruption that briefly spikes spot prices."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: true only if a major USD spot Bitcoin market prints a price above $100,000 at any time from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31, not merely if sentiment, futures, intraday rumors, or non-major venues imply it. Starting near the mid-$40,000s means the threshold requires a bit more than a 2x move within one calendar year. My base rate for Bitcoin years starting after a strong recovery and facing a major cycle catalyst is roughly 30% for exceeding a further doubling threshold within the year: Bitcoin has a history of very large rallies, but also frequent failed breakouts and severe drawdowns. I update upward because 2024 had two unusually clear upside catalysts in the evidence packet: widely anticipated US spot ETF approvals and the expected April halving. Those mechanisms could create both new demand and a narrative feedback loop. I do not update higher because ETF approval was not yet complete, much of it may have been anticipated, the halving timing leaves only part of the year for post-halving effects, and a move above $100,000 likely needs several favorable conditions at once: sustained ETF inflows, macro support, risk appetite, and no major crypto-specific shock. The inside-view chain points to a real but minority chance: catalyst arrival is plausible, but the magnitude and timing needed to more than double before 2025 are demanding.
attempts.strongest_yes: The strongest yes case is that 2024 combines two powerful mechanisms: anticipated spot ETF approval could bring a new structural demand channel, while the April halving supplies a familiar scarcity narrative. In a reflexive asset like Bitcoin, sustained inflows plus momentum could plausibly turn a move from the mid-$40,000s to above $100,000 into a same-year overshoot rather than a slow fundamental repricing.
attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no case is that the threshold is far away and conjunctive. Bitcoin needs more than ETF approval; it needs sustained inflows, favorable macro conditions, continued risk appetite, and avoidance of the frequent drawdowns or failed breakouts that characterize prior cycles. If any one of those legs disappoints, Bitcoin can have a good year and still fail to exceed $100,000 before 2025.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether ETF approval actually occurs and how much demand it creates versus how much was already priced in.","Whether the halving effect is immediate enough to matter before 2025-01-01.","Whether macro liquidity and risk appetite support speculative assets throughout enough of 2024.","Whether Bitcoin experiences a normal but threshold-killing drawdown after a rally attempt.","How reflexive the market becomes if Bitcoin approaches prior highs and attracts momentum buying."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast is wrong on the low side, the likely cause is underestimating reflexivity: ETF approval attracts larger-than-expected inflows, the halving narrative concentrates attention, macro conditions cooperate, and price momentum itself creates the marginal buyer needed for a fast overshoot above $100,000. If it is wrong on the high side, the likely cause is treating catalysts as additive when they are correlated or already anticipated: approval arrives but inflows disappoint, the market sells the news, halving impact is delayed, and Bitcoin spends 2024 volatile but below the threshold.
attempts.wildcards: ["Unexpectedly large institutional allocation through ETFs or similar spot access vehicles.","A broad liquidity surge or risk-on macro regime that amplifies crypto beta.","A severe crypto-specific shock, exchange failure, regulatory action, or security event that triggers a large drawdown.","A rapid speculative mania if Bitcoin breaks key psychological levels and momentum accelerates."]

Starting from the mid-$40,000s, Bitcoin needs a little more than a double within one year. The reference class is prior Bitcoin years beginning after a large recovery and approaching a known supply/cycle catalyst, where large upside moves are possible but failed breakouts and large drawdowns are common. I put the base rate around 28%. The inside view is somewhat higher because ETF approval anticipation and the April halving are both plausible catalysts, but both may already be partly priced and the threshold requires not just good news but sustained inflows, macro support, and risk appetite. attempts.rationale: Resolution requires that, from the January 1, 2024 information state, Starliner's first crewed flight both launch with astronauts and return those same astronauts to Earth before 2025-01-01. I treat this as a conjunctive schedule-and-safety event: launch in 2024, no prelaunch certification or hardware blocker pushes it into 2025, the mission remains crewed, and the crew returns before year-end. A concrete base rate for long-delayed first crewed test flights publicly targeted for the coming year is only modestly favorable because final certification often exposes late blockers; I estimate 55%. It does not move much higher because the same program had a multi-year pattern of software, valve, parachute, and certification surprises, and crew safety rules can convert small unresolved issues into large schedule slips or return-plan changes. Multiplying rough legs gives about 70% for launch in 2024, 95% that it remains the first crewed flight with astronauts if it launches, and 92% that those astronauts return before 2025 conditional on launch, yielding roughly 61%.

The strongest fixed-evidence base rate is the United States winning the last four men's Olympic basketball gold medals listed in the packet, 2008, 2012, 2016, and 2020. A raw 4-for-4 rate is 100%, but that is too high for forecasting a single future tournament because the sample is small, the field has improved, and knockout variance matters. I shrink that historical dominance to an 85% base rate. The case-specific evidence moves down from that base rate because recent FIBA results showed vulnerability when top US stars did not play, international parity had increased, and France, Serbia, Canada, Germany, and others had NBA-level talent. It moves back up because the US talent pool remained the deepest and early signals suggested a star-heavy roster was likely after the 2023 World Cup disappointment. Net: the inside-view estimate is lower than the dynasty base rate but still clearly favorable, yielding a final forecast of 76%.

attempts.strongest_no: The event is not a season-long league table; it can hinge on one knockout game, and the evidence packet explicitly flags international parity, NBA-level opponents, roster dependence, health, and single-elimination variance.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether the star-heavy roster actually commits and remains intact.","Whether US roster talent translates into international rules, chemistry, and late-game execution.","How strong and healthy France, Serbia, Canada, Germany, and other challengers are.","Knockout draw and whether the US faces a particularly bad matchup in a single-elimination game.","Whether recent FIBA vulnerability reflects a real narrowing gap or mainly weaker US roster selection in non-Olympic tournaments."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast fails, the most likely story is not that the US lacked talent overall, but that one necessary leg broke: the roster was less dominant than expected, one or more core stars were unavailable or limited, the team had poor chemistry under international rules, or a strong opponent with NBA-level talent caught them in a single-elimination game. A second failure path is overreading the four-Olympic gold streak as a stable base rate despite a tiny sample and changing international talent distribution. Resolution ambiguity is low because the criterion is simply who won gold, but the hidden risk is that 'US is best on paper' is not the same as 'US wins the tournament.'
attempts.wildcards: ["Major injury or withdrawal by a central US player shortly before or during the Olympics.","An unusually strong opponent performance in a knockout game, especially hot three-point shooting.","US foul trouble, chemistry issues, or poor adaptation to international officiating and spacing.","A challenger assembling a near-best possible roster while the US roster underdelivers relative to expectations."]
attempts.cited_sources: [{"claim":"No external sources used; forecast is based only on the fixed evidence packet supplied in the prompt."}]
attempts.reference_class: Recent Olympic men's basketball tournaments in which the United States entered with access to the world's deepest talent pool, especially 2008, 2012, 2016, and 2020.
attempts.resolution_boundary: True only if the United States men's basketball team won the men's basketball gold medal at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games; false for silver, bronze, withdrawal, disqualification, or any other country winning gold.
attempts.evidence_for: ["The United States had won men's Olympic basketball gold in 2008, 2012, 2016, and 2020.","The US talent pool remained the deepest in the world.","Early signals suggested a star-heavy US roster was likely for Paris after disappointment at the 2023 World Cup."]
attempts.evidence_against: ["Recent FIBA tournaments showed increased international parity and US vulnerability when top stars did not play.","France, Serbia, Canada, Germany, and others had NBA-level talent.","Gold probability depended heavily on roster commitment, health, and single-elimination variance."]
attempts.calibration_warnings: ["The base rate is based on only four recent Olympic tournaments, so it is informative but statistically fragile.","Roster commitment is correlated with health, chemistry, and motivation; treating these as independent would overstate confidence.","The evidence packet lacks betting markets, exact roster lists, opponent rosters, draw information, and player health data as of 2024-01-01.","Recent FIBA weakness may be either a genuine parity signal or mostly a selection-effect signal from non-star US rosters.","Avoid anchoring too heavily on national talent depth because the resolution is a single tournament outcome."]
